metadata.title: The Teacher's Wrong Answers Are the Lesson

beats.narration_text: Yassou, Liam. This is Liam, in for Bear. To train a small network, the ground-truth labels are less useful than a big network's mistakes.

props.command: How do I train a small student model to match a much larger teacher model's performance?
output: Don't train on one-hot labels. Train on the teacher's soft outputs — the probability distribution that encodes which classes resemble each other.

beats.narration_text: The book's distillation pipeline: one image into the large teacher, same image into the small student. The soft-label probability distribution is the signal the student learns from — not just the hard label.

One-hot labels: cat equals 1, everything else equals 0. That's what the ground truth says. The label is binary and flat. It tells the network 'this is a cat' — and nothing else. Which other class was the second-best guess? The label doesn't say. What resembles a cat? The label doesn't care. This is the information you're throwing away.

The teacher network on the same image: cat — 85%. Dog — 5%. That 5% is the lesson. It encodes the fact that dogs and cats share visual structure — fur, ears, facial geometry. The teacher learned this from millions of examples. That terracotta bar is information the one-hot label has no way to represent. The student trained on soft labels inherits this structure without seeing the original data.

The student trains on a mixed loss. Part hard: match the ground truth label. Part soft: match the teacher's probability distribution. Temperature T equals 3 flattens the teacher output, making small probabilities — that 5% dog — more visible in the gradient. The student gains two to five percent accuracy over training from hard labels alone. Not from more data — from richer signal.

Your turn. Ask Claude: I want to distill a large open bracket teacher params close bracket M parameter teacher into a open bracket student params close bracket M student. Walk me through: one — what information the teacher's soft labels contain that one-hot labels don't. Two — how to set the temperature T and alpha for the mixed loss. Three — what accuracy gain to expect vs training the student from scratch. Four — whether I need access to the original training data.

props.command: I want to distill a large [teacher_params] M parameter teacher into a [student_params] M student. Walk me through: (1) what information the teacher's soft labels contain that one-hot labels don't, (2) how to set the temperature T and alpha for the mixed loss, (3) what accuracy gain to expect vs training the student from scratch, (4) whether I need access to the original training data.
output: Teacher: [0.85 cat, 0.05 dog, ...]. The 5% dog encodes visual similarity. Student gains 2-5% accuracy from soft labels vs hard-label training.

props.title: The Teacher's Wrong Answers Are the Lesson
